methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $350M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Quantum Machines

data.narrative:
Oxford Quantum Circuits, a Series C quantum computing company led by founder Peter Leek, has raised $350M to date. The valuation analysis draws from a comp universe of 11 peers: three public companies (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum) and eight private firms including Quantinuum and Infleqtion.

The stage-progression method applies a 1.7x multiplier to the $350M total raised, reflecting typical step-up dynamics for Series C advancement to the next funding round. This approach yields $595M. A peer-median analysis was not applicable due to insufficient datapoints: while seven same-sector peers exist, the narrow filters for stage alignment and valuation tier yielded fewer than three comparable observations, making sector-wide medians unreliable.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $506M (low) to $684M (high), centered on $595M (median). This single-method triangulation carries material uncertainty. The quantum computing sector remains nascent with limited pricing precedent at equivalent stages; peer comps span heterogeneous architectures and commercialization timelines. Series C quantum ventures typically face 18–36 month runways to Series D or strategic milestone, making execution risk a substantive driver of valuation. Key uncertainties include hardware demonstration progress, customer pipeline depth, and macroeconomic conditions affecting later-stage funding availability.
